name: Python
creator: Guido van Rossum
description: Python is a versatile high-level scripting language with a simple, readable syntax. It is widely used for web development, data science, automation, and education. Python's robust standard library and community support make it a preferred language for learners and professionals.
usedFor: Web applications (Django, Flask), data analysis (Pandas, NumPy), artificial intelligence, scripting, automation, and scientific research.
funFact: Python was named after the British comedy group Monty Python, not after the snake.

topics.title: Variables
topics.explanation: Variables are containers for storing data values. In Python, you don't need to declare the type of a variable before using it; Python will infer it automatically.

keyConcepts.definition: Setting a variable to a value using the '=' operator.
Python determines the type of variable at runtime.

topics.codeExample:
x = 10
y = 'Hello'
z = 3.14
print(x, y, z)
topics.challenge: Create a variable called 'name' and set it to your first name. Print it.
topics.hint: Use the assignment operator '=' and the print() function.

topics.title: Lists
topics.explanation: Lists are ordered collections of items that can be changed (mutable). They're used to store sequences of data.

keyConcepts.definition: The content can be changed after creation.
Accessing elements by their position in the list.

topics.challenge: Create a list of three numbers and print the last one.
topics.hint: Use square brackets and remember index -1 gives the last element.

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.explanation: Dictionaries store key-value pairs. They're useful for associating a value with a unique key.

keyConcepts.definition: The unique identifier in a dictionary.
The data or object associated with a key.

commonMistakes: Accessing keys that do not exist.
Using mutable types (like lists) as dictionary keys.
topics.challenge: Create a dictionary for a car with keys 'make' and 'year', then print the year.
topics.hint: Use curly braces and strings for the keys.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform a task. They help organize code and avoid repetition.

keyConcepts.definition: Creating a function with the 'def' keyword.
Executing the function.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to indent the function body.
Forgetting to use parentheses when calling.
topics.challenge: Write a function that takes one number as input and prints it doubled.
topics.hint: Use def and print(num * 2) inside the function.

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: Classes let you build your own types of objects. They group together variables (attributes) and functions (methods) in a logical way.

keyConcepts.definition: A blueprint for objects.
An individual object of a class.

topics.challenge: Create a class "Book" with a title attribute, and print the title.

topics.title: File Handling
topics.explanation: Python can read and write files. This is helpful for saving and loading data from disk.

keyConcepts.definition: Use open() function to access files.
Specify mode: 'r' to read, 'w' to write.

topics.codeExample:
with open('sample.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Hello World!')
commonMistakes: Forgetting to close files (unless using with statement).
topics.hint: Use with open('data.txt', 'w') as f: f.write('Data')

topics.title: List Comprehension
topics.explanation: List comprehensions provide a concise way to create lists. They are more readable and faster than loops for making new lists.

keyConcepts.definition: A compact way to generate data structures.

topics.codeExample:
squares = [x*x for x in range(5)]
print(squares)
commonMistakes: Misunderstanding the order of the for and if clauses.
topics.challenge: Use a list comprehension to create a list of numbers from 1 to 5, each multiplied by 3.
topics.solution:
result = [x * 3 for x in range(1, 6)]
print(result)

topics.title: Decorators
topics.explanation: Decorators are special functions that can modify other functions. They're used to add functionality without changing the original code.

keyConcepts.definition: A function that modifies the behavior of another function.
Use '@' before the decorator function.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to return the wrapper function.
topics.challenge: Write a decorator that prints 'Start' before the function and 'End' after.
topics.hint: Follow the wrapper pattern and add print statements before and after.

name: JavaScript
creator: Brendan Eich
description: JavaScript is the language of the web, running in all modern browsers. It's used to create interactive websites and web applications. JavaScript now also powers backend servers and desktop apps.
usedFor: Client-side web development, web applications, server-side scripting (Node.js), mobile app development, desktop apps.
funFact: JavaScript was created in just 10 days at Netscape.

topics.title: Variables
topics.explanation: Variables hold data in JavaScript. 'let' and 'const' are modern ways to declare variables; 'var' is older.

keyConcepts.definition: Declares a variable with block scope.
Declares a constant variable; its value cannot be reassigned.

topics.challenge: Declare a constant called 'color' and set it to 'blue'. Log it to the console.

topics.title: Arrays
topics.explanation: Arrays are lists that store multiple values. They are indexed from 0, and you can use built-in methods to add, remove, or modify elements.

keyConcepts.definition: An ordered collection of items.
The position of an element in an array.

commonMistakes: Modifying arrays declared as 'const' (allowed for content, not reassignment).
topics.challenge: Create an array of three cities and log the second one.
topics.hint: Remember JavaScript arrays start at index 0.

topics.title: Objects
topics.explanation: Objects group key-value pairs. They are used to store structured data.

keyConcepts.definition: A variable contained in an object.
A function stored as a property.

commonMistakes: Using incorrect syntax for object literals.
topics.challenge: Make an object for a laptop with brand and RAM properties, then log the RAM.
topics.hint: Use curly braces and colon syntax.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Functions organize code into reusable blocks. You declare functions and call them where needed.

keyConcepts.definition: Defines a named function.
The value a function gives back when it's done.

commonMistakes: Not returning a value when needed.
topics.challenge: Write a function that adds two numbers and returns the sum.

topics.title: Loops
topics.explanation: Loops repeat code multiple times. JavaScript provides for, while, and forEach for looping.

keyConcepts.definition: Repeats a block a set number of times.
Each pass through the loop.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the loop variable.
Creating infinite loops by missing exit condition.
topics.challenge: Write a for loop that logs numbers from 1 to 5.

topics.title: Document Object Model (DOM)
topics.explanation: The DOM lets JavaScript interact with and update HTML on a web page, enabling dynamic content.

keyConcepts.definition: Represents the web page loaded in the browser.
Finds an HTML element with a specific id.

commonMistakes: Running DOM code before the page is loaded.
topics.challenge: Change the text of an element with ID 'welcome' to 'Hi there!' using JS.

topics.title: Events
topics.explanation: Events let you run code in response to user actions, like clicks or key presses.

keyConcepts.definition: Attach a function to run when an event happens.

commonMistakes: Referencing incorrect or missing element IDs.
topics.challenge: Add a click event to an element with id 'go' that logs 'Go clicked!'.

topics.title: Promises
topics.explanation: Promises handle asynchronous actions in JS, like loading data from a server. They can be pending, fulfilled, or rejected.

keyConcepts.definition: An object representing a task that completes in the future.
Method for handling successful results.

commonMistakes: Not returning the promise in functions.
topics.challenge: Create a promise that resolves with 'Success' and prints the result.
topics.hint: Use new Promise, resolve, and then().
